verses.text: Satan provokes David to number Israel, and even Joab protests, 'why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel?' The census sprang from pride in his own strength. To trust in numbers rather than in God is a snare the adversary loves to set.

Satan provokes David to number Israel, and even Joab protests, 'why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel?' The census sprang from pride in his own strength. To trust in numbers rather than in God is a snare the adversary loves to set.

Satan provokes David to number Israel, and even Joab protests, 'why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel?' The census sprang from pride in his own strength. To trust in numbers rather than in God is a snare the adversary loves to set.

Joab carries out the count against his own judgment, but will not number Levi and Benjamin, for 'the king's word was abominable to Joab.' The reluctant obedience marks the sin. When even a hardened servant recoils, a leader should pause and repent.

God is displeased and smites Israel, and David confesses, 'I have sinned greatly... I have done very foolishly.' He owns the sin plainly. True repentance makes no excuse but casts itself wholly on the mercy of God.

God is displeased and smites Israel, and David confesses, 'I have sinned greatly... I have done very foolishly.' He owns the sin plainly. True repentance makes no excuse but casts itself wholly on the mercy of God.

Offered three judgments, David chooses to fall 'into the hand of the LORD; for very great are his mercies: but let me not fall into the hand of man.' Faith reasons rightly even under the rod: better God's chastening than man's cruelty.

Offered three judgments, David chooses to fall 'into the hand of the LORD; for very great are his mercies: but let me not fall into the hand of man.' Faith reasons rightly even under the rod: better God's chastening than man's cruelty.

A plague kills seventy thousand; as the angel reaches Jerusalem, David sees him and pleads, 'these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand be on me.' The shepherd-king offers himself for his people — a faint shadow of the Shepherd who bore the stroke for His flock.

Gad bids David build an altar at Ornan's threshingfloor, where the angel stood, and Ornan offers it freely. The place of judgment is to become the place of worship. God turns the spot of dread into a meeting place of mercy.

Gad bids David build an altar at Ornan's threshingfloor, where the angel stood, and Ornan offers it freely. The place of judgment is to become the place of worship. God turns the spot of dread into a meeting place of mercy.

Gad bids David build an altar at Ornan's threshingfloor, where the angel stood, and Ornan offers it freely. The place of judgment is to become the place of worship. God turns the spot of dread into a meeting place of mercy.

David refuses to take it free: 'I will not offer... that which doth cost me nothing,' pays the full price, builds the altar, and God answers by fire from heaven. Worship that costs nothing is worth nothing; and this very floor would become the temple's site.

The LORD commands the angel to sheath his sword; the plague is stayed, though David, awed, still feared to go to Gibeon. Mercy ends the judgment. The contrite who seek God at the altar of sacrifice find the sword of wrath put away.
